entries.title: Burp extensions - overview
entries.body:
# Burp extensions - overview

Extensions add functionality to Burp that isn't there out of the box. Through a programmatic API, an extension can hook into almost any stage of Burp's work: edit HTTP requests and responses on the fly, send its own requests, draw its own tabs and panels in the UI, add checks to Burp Scanner, react to scanner and proxy events. In essence it's a way to tailor Burp to a specific methodology or target.

## Where extensions come from

There are two sources:

- **BApp Store** - a built-in store of ready-made extensions from the community. They install in a couple of clicks right from Burp. PortSwigger reviews the code before publication, but the extensions are still third-party - there are no quality guarantees, and it's worth reviewing the code yourself.
- **Your own development** - you write an extension for your task and load it from a file.

## Two APIs

- **Montoya API** - the modern, actively maintained interface for extensions in Java (and Kotlin). Everything new is written on it.
- **Legacy Extender API** - the deprecated interface. It is the one that provides support for Python (via Jython) and Ruby (via JRuby), but no new features appear in it. Use it only if there's no other way.

## What to read next

The section tree walks you through typical tasks:

- **installation** - from the BApp Store and manually from a file;
- **management** - enabling/disabling, processing order, viewing output and errors;
- **troubleshooting** - what to do when an extension won't load or runs slowly;
- **development** - setting up the environment, your first extension, tutorials, debugging, and loading into Burp.

> If the task is simple (your own filter/column or a check for the scanner), a full-blown extension may be overkill - look toward Bambdas and BChecks. They're simpler and don't require building a JAR.

entries.title: Installing extensions - overview
entries.body:
# Installing extensions - overview

You can install an extension in Burp two ways, and the choice depends on where you got it.

## From the BApp Store

The built-in store: you open **Extensions > BApp Store**, find what you need, and install it with a single **Install** button. The most convenient way for ready-made community extensions - Burp downloads and connects the file itself.

## Manually from a file

This is the right approach when:

- you're **offline** and can't reach the BApp Store;
- the extension is **self-written** or obtained elsewhere (your own `.jar`, someone else's `.py`/`.rb`);
- you downloaded a `.bapp` file from the BApp Store in advance via a browser.

Loading is done through **Extensions > Installed > Add**.

## Important about Python and Ruby

Java extensions work right away. But to load a **Python** extension, you first need to connect **Jython** (a separate standalone JAR), and for **Ruby** - **JRuby** (also a JAR). Without the configured environment, the corresponding type will be unavailable in the add dialog. For where to specify the path to these JARs, see the extension management page and troubleshooting.

> The order is the same: first (if needed) you set up the Jython/JRuby environment, then you add the extension itself.

entries.title: Installing from the BApp Store
Manual installation from a file
entries.body:
# Manual installation from a file

When you have an extension as a file - a self-written `.jar`, someone else's `.py`/`.rb`, or a pre-downloaded `.bapp` - you connect it manually.

## First - the environment for Python/Ruby

For a **Python** extension you first need to set up **Jython**, for **Ruby** - **JRuby** (both connect as a standalone JAR). Without this, the needed type will be unavailable in the dialog. For exactly how to specify the path to the JAR, see extension troubleshooting.

## Connecting your extension

1. Open **Extensions > Installed** and click **Add**.
2. In the dialog, in the **Extension type** list select the type: **Java**, **Python**, or **Ruby**.
3. Click **Select file** and point to the extension file (`.jar`, `.py`, or `.rb`).
4. If desired, configure where to direct **Standard output** and **Standard error**.
5. Click **Next** - Burp will load the extension.
6. Review the messages on the **Output** and **Errors** tabs (there you can see whether the extension came up and what errors there were).
7. Click **Close**.

The extension will enable automatically and go to the end of the list of installed ones.

## Importing a downloaded .bapp

If you downloaded an extension file from the BApp Store in advance (for example, for an offline machine):

1. Open **Extensions > BApp Store**.
2. Click the menu button and select **Import BApp file**.
3. Point to the downloaded `.bapp` file and click **Open**.

The extension will install and enable on its own.

> Self-written extensions are convenient to load with auto-reload on file change enabled - you rebuild the JAR, and Burp picks up the new version without manual fuss (see extension management).

entries.title: Managing extensions
entries.body:
# Managing extensions

All installed extensions live on the **Extensions > Installed** tab as a table. From here you enable, disable, reorder, and remove them.

## The extensions table

Columns:

- **Loaded** - the "loaded" checkbox. Clear it to temporarily disable an extension without removing it from the list; check it back to enable.
- **Type** - the type (Java / Python / Ruby).
- **Name** - the extension name.

Buttons and controls:

- **Up** / **Down** - move the extension in the list. Order matters: extensions process traffic top to bottom, and for chains where one extension changes what another sees, the order is significant.
- **Remove** - unload and remove the extension from the list.
- **Auto-reload** - auto-reload of manually loaded extensions on file change (handy during development).
- **Extensions settings** - settings: startup behavior, extension folders, and the **Jython**/**JRuby** environments (the path to the standalone Python and Ruby JAR is set right here).

## Panels below the table

Below the list are three tabs with data on the selected extension:

- **Details** - general information;
- **Output** - what the extension writes to standard output;
- **Errors** - exceptions and errors.

## Useful tricks

- Disabling via clearing **Loaded** doesn't lose settings - the extension stays in the list.
- To manually reload an extension, click the **Loaded** checkbox while holding Ctrl/Cmd.
- If there are many extensions, keep the order meaningful: what should edit traffic first goes higher.

> When something goes wrong (an extension won't load, conflicts, or runs slowly), start with the **Errors** tab and the troubleshooting page.

entries.title: Troubleshooting extensions
entries.body:
# Troubleshooting extensions

If an extension won't install, won't load, or Burp started running slowly - here are the typical causes and what to do.

## The BApp Store won't open

- **Offline / no network** - the store needs internet. Solution: download the `.bapp` via a browser on a machine with access and install it manually.
- **An upstream proxy is needed** - if network access is only through a corporate proxy, configure it in Burp's settings.
- **Intercepting proxy with a self-signed CA** - if SSL inspection is in place on the network, Burp may not trust its certificate.

## An extension won't load

- **Old version of Burp** - the extension needs API features your build doesn't have yet. Update Burp.
- **No Jython/JRuby** - for Python/Ruby extensions you need to set up the environment. Download the standalone JAR (Jython for Python, JRuby for Ruby) and specify it in **Settings > Extensions** in the **Python Environment** or **Ruby Environment** section.
- **Community Edition** - the extension uses features available only in Professional.

## Slowdowns and load

- Several extensions together can consume a lot of resources.
- **How to localize the culprit**: disable all extensions (clear **Loaded**), then enable them one at a time, watching the load.
- In the BApp Store each extension has an **Estimated system impact** with ratings for **Memory**, **CPU**, **Time**, **Scanner** - check it before installing heavy ones.

## Metaspace memory error

With several Python/Ruby extensions you may hit:

```
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace
```

This is fixed by allocating Java a larger Metaspace via a startup parameter:

```
-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=1G
```

## Where to look and who to turn to

- Error details are on the extension's **Errors** (and **Output**) tab.
- For support on a specific extension, go to its GitHub repository to the author.

> Before writing to the author, it doesn't hurt to run the extension's code through an AI - often the cause is visible right away.

entries.title: Developing extensions - overview
entries.body:
# Developing extensions - overview

If there's no ready-made extension for the task, you write your own. PortSwigger explicitly recommends: **extensions in Java using the Montoya API**. This API is actively developed, well documented, and designed to be convenient.

## What to write in

- **Java + Montoya API** - the main path. Modern, supported.
- **Kotlin** - also fits, compiles into the same `.jar`.
- **Python (Jython) / Ruby (JRuby)** - available only through the deprecated Extender API, which is no longer developed. Use only if absolutely necessary.

## Resources

- The Montoya API **JavaDoc** - a full description of the interfaces and methods.
- The `burp-extensions-montoya-api` examples repository on GitHub - working sample extensions.
- The starter project includes a `CLAUDE.md` file - a template for AI-assisted development.

## Development route

1. **Set up the development environment**.
2. **Write your first extension** (Hello, world).
3. **Work through the tutorials** on specific features.
4. **Set up remote debugging**.
5. **Load the extension into Burp**.
6. (optional) **Submit to the BApp Store** and then **maintain** it.

## When an extension is overkill

For simple scenarios there are lighter tools:

- **Bambdas** - short scripts for your own filters and columns;
- **BChecks** - your own checks for the scanner.

> A full-blown extension is justified when you need your own UI, complex logic, or integration. For "tweaking a filter" a Bambda is enough.

entries.title: Setting up the development environment
entries.body:
# Setting up the development environment

Before writing an extension, you need to assemble a working environment: an IDE, a build, and a dependency on the Montoya API. There are two paths.

## Two ways

- **Starter project (recommended)** - a ready-made Java project with a configured build, a connected dependency, and a code template. It downloads right from Burp: **Extensions > APIs > Download starter project**. A quick start with no manual fuss.
- **Manual setup** - you assemble the project from scratch when you need full control over the configuration.

## Common requirements

- **IDE**: **IntelliJ IDEA** is recommended (the Community Edition is enough) - it picks up Gradle and configures the project itself.
- **Build**: **Gradle** (in the starter project - Kotlin DSL, the `build.gradle.kts` and `settings.gradle.kts` files); Maven is supported too.
- **Java version - 21**. In the IDE the parameter may be called **JDK**, **SDK**, or **JRE**. Important: Burp runs extensions on **Java 21 or lower** - newer bytecode won't load.

## The Montoya API dependency

The dependency is connected from Maven Central. Coordinates:

```
groupId:    net.portswigger.burp.extensions
artifactId: montoya-api
```

Montoya API versions are dated (like `2025.2`, `2026.x`) - take the current one from the GitHub releases. In the starter project everything is already specified.

## What's next

The details of both paths are on separate pages:

- setup via the **starter project**;
- **manual** setup.

> If you're just starting out, take the starter project. Leave the manual build for the case where the standard configuration doesn't fit.

entries.title: Starter project and manual setup
entries.body:
# Starter project and manual setup

Two ways to get a ready-to-build extension project: take a template from PortSwigger or assemble the configuration by hand.

## Starter project (the fast path)

1. In Burp open **Extensions > APIs > Download starter project** and download the archive.
2. Unpack it and open the folder in **IntelliJ IDEA** - it will detect Gradle and configure the project itself.
3. Set the project's Java version to **21** (the parameter may be called **JDK** / **SDK** / **JRE**).
4. The main file is `Extension.java`: the class implements the `BurpExtension` interface and contains the `initialize(MontoyaApi montoyaApi)` method. This is where the logic is written.

The Montoya API dependency and the fat-JAR build are already configured in the starter project - you can build and load into Burp right away.

## Manual setup

If you need your own project from scratch, connect the Montoya API dependency from Maven Central.

**Gradle (Kotlin DSL, `build.gradle.kts`):**

```kotlin
dependencies {
    compileOnly("net.portswigger.burp.extensions:montoya-api:LATEST")
}
```

**Gradle (Groovy, `build.gradle`):**

```groovy
dependencies {
    compileOnly 'net.portswigger.burp.extensions:montoya-api:LATEST'
}
```

**Maven (`pom.xml`):**

```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>net.portswigger.burp.extensions</groupId>
    <artifactId>montoya-api</artifactId>
    <version>LATEST</version>
</dependency>
```

Instead of `LATEST`, substitute the current dated version (for example, `2026.x`) from the releases page on GitHub. The API is needed only at compile time - hence `compileOnly` (it doesn't need to be embedded in the JAR; Burp provides the API itself).

## Important points

- The Java version is **21 or lower**, otherwise the extension won't load.
- If you pull in third-party libraries, they **must be embedded in the JAR** (fat/shadow jar), otherwise the extension won't find the classes at runtime. In the starter project this is already done; with a manual build it's configured separately (see loading into Burp).

> The `net.portswigger.burp.extensions:montoya-api` coordinates are the single source of truth for the dependency. Keep the version fresh so the new API methods are available.

entries.title: Your first extension (Hello, world)
entries.body:
# Your first extension (Hello, world)

A minimal extension on the Montoya API comes down to one class and one method. Let's break down the skeleton and a couple of basic features.

## Entry point

The extension class implements the `BurpExtension` interface and overrides the `initialize(MontoyaApi montoyaApi)` method. Burp calls it on load and passes a `MontoyaApi` object - through it everything else is accessible.

```java
public class Extension implements BurpExtension {
    @Override
    public void initialize(MontoyaApi montoyaApi) {
        // configure the extension here
    }
}
```

## Extension name

It's set like this - this is exactly the name visible in the **Extensions > Installed** list:

```java
montoyaApi.extension().setName("My first extension");
```

## Output to the log

The message goes to the extension's **Output** tab:

```java
montoyaApi.logging().logToOutput("Hello, world!");
```

## Context menu item

To add your own item to the right-click menu, you register an implementation of `ContextMenuItemsProvider`. Its `provideMenuItems()` method returns a list of `JMenuItem` that Burp shows in the appropriate context.

## Your own tab

You assemble a `JPanel` with Swing components and add it as a Burp tab:

```java
montoyaApi.userInterface().registerSuiteTab("Tab Title", panel);
```

## What the example demonstrates

The resulting "Hello, world!" extension combines everything listed: it sets a name, writes to Output, registers a context menu item with a handler, and adds its own tab with a label. This is the template that more complex extensions build on.

> The key Montoya objects - `extension()`, `logging()`, `userInterface()`, `http()`, `scanner()`, etc. - are all taken from the passed `MontoyaApi`. For the full list see the JavaDoc.

entries.title: Montoya API tutorials
entries.body:
# Montoya API tutorials

After the first "Hello, world" come practical tutorials on specific features. They're aimed at those who have already set up the environment and built the starter extension.

## Available tutorials

- **Adding a settings panel** - adding your own settings panel. It shows how to give an extension its own options and store them (persistent settings) so the values survive a restart.
- **Adding a hotkey** - assigning a hotkey that launches your extension's action.

## Related features

Beyond the tutorials themselves, the development section also covers other directions:

- **Custom scan checks** - your own checks for the scanner (passive and active);
- **Bambdas** - short scripts for your own actions, filters, and AI features;
- **AI extensions** - extensions with AI capabilities.

> Tutorials are "recipes" for typical tasks. You take the appropriate one, repeat it on your own project, and then build out the logic. If a feature isn't in a tutorial, look for it in the JavaDoc and the examples on GitHub.

entries.title: Remote debugging of an extension
entries.body:
# Remote debugging of an extension

You can debug an extension by connecting the IDE's debugger to a running Burp over the JDWP protocol. This lets you see code execution in real time, with breakpoints and variable inspection.

## The JVM startup argument for Burp

Launch Burp from the JAR with the debug agent. By default port **5005** is used:

```
java -agentlib:jdwp=transport=dt_socket,server=y,suspend=n,address=*:5005 -jar "FILE_PATH.jar"
```

Here `FILE_PATH.jar` is the path to Burp's JAR file. `suspend=n` means Burp starts immediately, without waiting for the debugger to connect. You can pick a different port - the main thing is that it matches the setting in the IDE and is free.

## Setup in the IDE

1. In the IDE (**IntelliJ IDEA** is recommended) create a **Remote JVM Debug** configuration (remote debugging).
2. The host is `localhost`, the port is the same **5005** (or yours).
3. Launch Burp with the command above - it starts and listens for the debugger to connect.
4. In the IDE run the created debug configuration - it will connect to Burp's JVM.
5. Build and load the extension into Burp, set breakpoints.

## To make everything work

- Run Burp and the IDE on the same machine if possible.
- For the duration of debugging, disable the other extensions so they don't interfere.
- If breakpoints don't trigger, rebuild and reload the extension: the version of the code in Burp must match the source in the IDE.
- Check that the port is free and not blocked.

> The combination `address=*:5005` + Remote JVM Debug on `localhost:5005` is a working baseline. Auto-reload of the extension on file change helps keep the versions in sync.

entries.title: Loading an extension into Burp
entries.body:
# Loading an extension into Burp

When the code is ready, the extension is built into a JAR and connected to Burp.

## Building the JAR

**Gradle:** from the project root run

```
./gradlew jar      # Linux/macOS
gradlew jar        # Windows
```

The finished file will appear in `<project_root>/build/libs/<project_name>.jar`.

**Maven:** from the project root

```
mvn clean package
```

The JAR will land in `<project_root>/target/<project_name>-<version>.jar`.

**Third-party libraries.** If the extension pulls in external dependencies, they need to be embedded in the JAR (fat/shadow jar) - otherwise at runtime the classes won't be found. In the starter project this is already configured; with a manual build a block is added to the build configuration (for example, `tasks.jar`).

## Connecting in Burp

1. Open **Extensions > Installed**.
2. Click **Add**.
3. Make sure the type is **Java**.
4. Click **Select file**.
5. Select the built `.jar` and click **Open**.
6. For development it's convenient to enable **Reload extension automatically when file changes** - Burp will pick up the new build itself.
7. If desired, configure where to direct **Output** and **Errors**.
8. Click **Next** - the extension will load.
9. Check the messages on the **Output** and **Errors** tabs.
10. Click **Close**.

The extension will appear in the table - you can test it.

## Auto-reload during development

In the extensions list there's an **Auto-reload** option: with it Burp reloads the extension every time you rebuild the JAR. This sharply speeds up the "edit -> build -> check" cycle - you don't have to manually remove and re-add the extension.

> The development work cycle: you edit the code in the IDE -> `gradlew jar` -> Burp with auto-reload enabled picks up the new JAR -> you check. With debugging, a connected Remote JVM Debug is added to this.
